chemicals.concentration_notes: Found in all parts of the plant.
chemicals.description: Various alkaloids that depress the central nervous system.
chemicals.name: Alkaloids (including opiates)

description: Poppy (Papaver spp.) is toxic to cats due to the presence of isoquinoline alkaloids — including morphine, codeine, papaverine, and related opioid compounds in opium poppy (Papaver somniferum), and non-opioid isoquinolines in other species. All parts of the plant contain these alkaloids, with the highest concentration in the seed pod latex.
name: Poppy
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: isoquinoline alkaloids (opioid and non-opioid types depending on species); seed pod latex most concentrated
Mechanism: opioid alkaloids bind to mu-opioid receptors, causing CNS and respiratory depression; cats are particularly sensitive to opioids
Symptoms: sedation, lethargy, pin-point pupils, slow or laboured breathing, loss of coordination, vomiting; severe cases cause respiratory failure
Even ornamental (non-opium) poppy species contain irritant alkaloids that cause GI upset
Any suspected poppy ingestion warrants prompt veterinary evaluation due to opioid sensitivity in cats

symptoms.name: Dilated pupils
symptoms.notes: This is a direct result of the plant's toxins affecting the central nervous system, assumed based on data from other mammals1. 2. Vomiting

symptoms.name: Vomiting
symptoms.notes: Extrapolated from symptoms seen in dogs and other mammals1. 3. Lack of Coordination

symptoms.name: Lack of coordination
symptoms.notes: Caused by the plant's impact on the nervous system; assumed to occur in cats based on mammalian data1. 4. Collapse

symptoms.name: Collapse
symptoms.notes: This represents a significant disruption of the nervous system and is extrapolated from reports in dogs and other mammals

treatments.description: Contact your veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately for tailored guidance, letting them know exactly what type of plant the cat was exposed to
treatments.name: Veterinary Consultation and Triage
treatments.notes: You should act quickly and not wait for clinical signs of poisoning (such as vomiting or lack of coordination) to appear before seeking professional help4. 2. Gastrointestinal Decontamination

treatments.description: To prevent the plant material from being fully digested and absorbed into the bloodstream, a veterinarian may medically induce vomiting or perform a gastric lavage (stomach pump)3. This is often followed by administering doses of activated charcoal to bind and neutralize any remaining toxins in the gastrointestinal tract
treatments.name: Emesis Induction, Gastric Lavage, and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: You should never attempt to induce vomiting at home unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinary professional, as doing it incorrectly can cause severe harm56. Furthermore, if the cat is already showing severe neurological signs like collapse1, the vet may need to bypass inducing vomiting

treatments.description: The cat will likely need to be hospitalized so the veterinary team can administer IV fluids to treat dehydration from vomiting, stabilize the animal, and help flush the toxins from the system37. The vet may also administer anti-nausea injections (antiemetics) and stomach-protecting medications
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Symptomatic Medication
treatments.notes: Because poppy toxicity targets the nervous system and causes incoordination and collapse1, the cat will need to be kept as comfortable as possible in a safe, confined space at the clinic to prevent self-injury while the neurological effects of the toxin slowly wear off
